text:
We introduce a novel class of models constructed from graphs containing undirected, directed, and bidirected connections. These models are termed marginal AMP (MAMP) chain graphs, as each can be Markov equivalent to some AMP chain graph following the marginalization of particular nodes. Beyond generalizing AMP chain graphs, MAMP chain graphs also encompass multivariate regression chain graphs. We establish global and pairwise Markov properties specific to MAMP chain graphs and demonstrate their equivalence within compositional graphoids. Additionally, we specify the conditions under which two MAMP chain graphs exhibit Markov equivalence.

For Gaussian probability distributions, we further demonstrate that any MAMP chain graph has a Markov equivalent representation as a directed acyclic graph incorporating deterministic nodes, through marginalization and conditioning on select nodes. This finding carries significant implications, revealing that the independence structure captured by a MAMP chain graph corresponds to a partially observed data generation mechanism subject to selection bias. We then refine MAMP chain graphs to ensure closure under marginalization in the Gaussian case. This refinement proves valuable as it ensures models remain parsimonious when nodes are marginalized away.
label: ai

text:
During the opening years of the 2000s, the initial comprehensive mathematical framework for general artificial intelligence emerged. Universal Artificial Intelligence (UAI), this novel framework, has yielded important advances across theoretical, philosophical, and applied domains within AI research. Through multiple publications culminating in a seminal work (Hutter, 2005), researchers constructed and thoroughly examined a rigorous mathematical model representing a maximally intelligent agent called AIXI. Although contemporary AI practitioners tend to sidestep the topic of intelligence itself, a celebrated dissertation (Legg, 2008) furnished the philosophical foundation and explored a UAI-derived objective measure of rational intelligence that remains both mathematically precise and independent of human-centric assumptions. Practical implementations of AIXI have been recently created and tested in a prominent journal (Veness et al. 2011). This practical advance has generated impressive real-world systems, helping to refute earlier objections claiming UAI lacks practical utility. Without requiring specialized knowledge about particular domains, the identical system can flexibly adjust itself to varied interactive settings. Consider how AIXI can acquire the ability to play TicTacToe, Pacman, Kuhn Poker, and comparable games through experimentation alone, requiring no explicit instruction about game mechanics.

These developments rekindle optimism that the ambitious vision of Artificial General Intelligence remains achievable.

This essay surveys UAI and its broader significance. It seeks to make an inherently abstract, rigorous, and mathematical topic accessible, examining its conceptual underpinnings, mathematical fundamentals, attributes characterizing intelligence, relevant societal concerns, and both its history and trajectory.
label: ai

We present compelling reasoning demonstrating that the resulting AIXI framework represents the most unbiased intelligent agent conceivable. We explain how AIXI can formally address numerous categories of challenges, spanning from forecasting sequences and competitive interactions to optimizing objectives and tackling both reinforcement-based and supervised training tasks. A significant limitation emerges in that AIXI cannot be practically computed. To address this computational barrier, we develop AIXItl, a revised variant maintaining superior intelligence relative to any competing agent constrained by computation duration t and description length l. The algorithmic complexity of AIXItl scales as t × 2^l. Our examination encompasses rigorous characterizations of cognitive capacity hierarchies, the temporal limitation problem, and connections between AIXI and competing intelligent system methodologies.

text: We introduce a novel family of quantum computational methods that extend numerous existing approaches. These algorithms aim to determine probability distributions, which are valuable in domains such as Decision Theory and Artificial Intelligence, where conclusions must be drawn from imperfect or incomplete information. Our proposed family relies on a construction technique that extends the Fredkin-Toffoli (F-T) approach, a well-established method in classical reversible computing. The F-T technique demonstrates that any binary deterministic circuit can be transformed into an equivalent binary deterministic circuit performing identical operations in a reversible fashion. We demonstrate an analogous transformation: starting from any classical stochastic network (classical Bayesian net), one can build a corresponding quantum network (quantum Bayesian net). When executed on quantum hardware, this quantum Bayesian net enables the computation of arbitrary conditional probabilities that would be obtainable from the source classical Bayesian net. In this way, we expand the F-T construction approach to encompass arbitrary classical stochastic circuits, moving beyond the limitation to binary deterministic circuits alone. Additionally, we illustrate that under specific conditions, our algorithmic family can be integrated with Grover's algorithm to achieve significant computational benefits.

text: Problems involving constraint satisfaction (CSPs) have received considerable attention across multiple disciplines including artificial intelligence, database systems, graph theory, and physics-based modeling. By contrast, we establish a dichotomy result for approximate enumeration in Boolean CSPs with complex-valued weights, assuming unrestricted access to all complex-valued unary constraints. The availability of these unrestricted unary constraints furnishes sufficient expressive capability to establish such a comprehensive, definitive classification. This advance represents progress toward achieving a complete approximation-complexity taxonomy of all counting CSPs. Addressing complex-valued weights requires employing decomposition techniques and degree-reduction arguments adapted from work on Holant-type problems. We further introduce T-constructibility as a novel framework that naturally generates approximation-preserving reductions. text:
A central challenge in artificial life research involves developing systems capable of unbounded evolutionary growth. This work investigates two significant variables affecting this challenge through experiments conducted within the Tierra platform.

One key variable is {\em complexity penalty}, which discourages the evolution of intricate life forms by imposing reproduction costs proportional to genome length, thereby biasing the system toward streamlined designs. Within Tierra, the \verb+SlicePow+ parameter governs this penalty mechanism. When maximum penalty is applied, evolutionary processes drive the ancestral strain toward minimal simplicity. Conversely, when penalty is minimized, organisms tend to expand in size without becoming genuinely more sophisticated, accumulating non-functional genetic material instead. This investigation explores varying \verb+SlicePow+ settings between 0.9 and 1.0 to identify whether an optimal configuration exists for fostering increased organizational sophistication.

Most artificial life platforms, including Tierra, rely on computational pseudo random sequences rather than authentic randomness. Since algorithms are fundamentally incapable of generating novel information and can only diminish it, Tierra's total system complexity remains constrained by its initial state—consequently limiting how complex individual organisms can become. Natural biological systems differ fundamentally, drawing on abundant genuine randomness sources ultimately rooted in quantum phenomena, thereby circumventing such complexity restrictions. Computer systems can access {\em entropy harvesters}—specialized tools that supply legitimate random data—and this research documents how substituting Tierra's pseudo random generator with an entropy harvester influences system behavior.
label: ai

text: Numerous scholars in the fields of artificial intelligence and cognitive science have contended that analogy forms the foundation of thought processes. To address this constraint, we present the Latent Relation Mapping Engine (LRME), which synthesizes methodologies from both SME and Latent Relational Analysis (LRA) to bypass the necessity of manually constructed representations. We test LRME against twenty analogical mapping tasks—half drawn from scientific contexts and half from conventional metaphorical expressions. text: The veto rule serves as a straightforward method for combining the choices of multiple decision-makers. text: The article introduces a methodology for establishing that certain problems are computationally intractable within the polynomial hierarchy or PSPACE complexity classes. text:

text: This work builds upon findings presented in two preceding studies. Earlier work [J. Schubert, On nonspecific evidence, Int. J. Intell. Syst. 8 (1993) 711-725] introduced the metaconflict function as a tool within Dempster-Shafer theory, enabling the division of multiple evidentiary items into groups where the associated propositions lack precise specification—that is, where uncertainty exists regarding which particular event a given proposition addresses. A subsequent investigation [J. Schubert, Specifying nonspecific evidence, in Cluster-based specification techniques in Dempster-Shafer theory for an evidential intelligence analysis of multiple target tracks, Ph.D. Thesis, TRITA-NA-9410, Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm, 1994, ISBN 91-7170-801-4] advanced this work by identifying not only the most probable grouping for each piece of evidence but also calculating the likelihood that any given piece of evidence belongs to each potential grouping. The present study pursues a different objective: determining a posterior probability distribution over the quantity of subsets. The approach rests on the principle that evidence within any subset bolsters the subset's existence to an extent proportional to the overall evidentiary strength of that evidence. Through this reasoning, a basic probability assignment can be constructed that addresses the determination of subset quantity. This assignment may subsequently be merged with a pre-established prior distribution over the domain to yield the desired posterior distribution.

text:
An earlier study [J. Schubert, On nonspecific evidence, Int. J. Intell.
Syst. 8(6), 711-725 (1993)] introduced the metaconflict function as a criterion
within Dempster-Shafer theory. This function enables the division of multiple
evidence items into subsets when their propositions lack specificity—that is,
when uncertainty exists regarding which event a given proposition addresses.
Each resulting subset corresponds to a distinct event. The metaconflict function
was conceptualized as the likelihood that the proposed partitioning holds true,
treating the conflict arising from Dempster's rule within each subset as a
newly generated metalevel piece of evidence whose proposition argues against the
entire partitioning scheme. The current work builds upon these earlier findings.
Rather than merely identifying the most probable subset for each evidence item,
as the previous work accomplished, we now go further. We determine the degree of
specificity for each nonspecific piece of evidence by calculating the likelihood
that it belongs to each possible subset, thereby revealing to which events the
proposition may pertain. As a byproduct, this approach identifies potentially
unreliable evidence. We subsequently introduce a novel framework for utilizing
these newly clarified evidence items in further analysis. This framework
encompasses techniques for adjusting the weight of evidence based on both
falsity likelihood and credibility stemming from incomplete specification of
affiliation, along with an enhanced procedure for determining each subset's
corresponding event.
label: ai

Its theoretical foundation posits that cultural change does not involve the propagation of memes or physical objects, but rather the underlying cognitive representations that produce them, advancing through a Lamarckian mechanism centered on the interchange of creative processes rather than through Darwinian competitive elimination. Within EVOC, the typical effectiveness of behaviors improves progressively, while behavioral variety initially expands before eventually contracting. The degree of behavioral variation correlates positively with both group scale and concentration, as well as with divisions separating different groups. Gradually softening boundaries between populations enhance behavioral effectiveness while sustaining variation by promoting the emergence of specialized practices followed by dissemination of superior behaviors. When a dominant figure broadcasts its behavioral patterns across the entire group, behavioral effectiveness rises but variation diminishes. Multiplying the quantity of influential figures lessens this outcome. Current investigations are examining scenarios in which an individual migrating between cultural contexts introduces innovative concepts while maintaining cultural compatibility.

text: Consider a scenario in which several domain experts or machine learning systems each propose distinct Bayesian network (BN) architectures. Our goal is to synthesize these proposals into a unified consensus BN architecture. The desired consensus structure should capture only those conditional independences that all proposed structures share while minimizing the quantity of associated model parameters. We demonstrate in this work that multiple non-isomorphic consensus BN structures can coexist and that computing any such structure constitutes an NP-hard problem. Given this computational difficulty, we turn to heuristic approaches as a practical alternative for obtaining approximate consensus BN architectures. This paper examines a particular heuristic strategy previously introduced in the literature, which relies on two core computational procedures—denoted Methods A and B—designed to efficiently compute the minimal directed independence map for a given BN structure under a specified node ordering. While these methods were presented as sound procedures, their correctness was never rigorously established; only informal justifications were provided. Our contribution demonstrates that both Methods A and B contain logical errors and presents corrected versions of these algorithms.

text: A standard Gaussian probability distribution representable through an AMP chain graph can be reformulated as a collection of linear relationships featuring correlated residuals, where the correlation pattern is determined by the chain graph's structure. Yet the chain graph encodes these residuals in an implicit manner, since no vertices within the chain graph serve to denote the residuals themselves. This paper proposes incorporating additional deterministic vertices into the chain graph to make the residuals explicit. The resulting structure is termed an EAMP chain graph. We demonstrate that each AMP chain graph maintains Markov equivalence with its corresponding EAMP chain graph once the error vertices are marginalized out. Furthermore, we establish that every EAMP chain graph, following marginalization of error vertices, is Markov equivalent to some LWF chain graph under similar marginalization, which in turn is Markov equivalent to a directed acyclic graph under marginalization of error vertices and conditioning on certain selection vertices. This finding is significant because it reveals that the independence relationships encoded by an AMP chain graph can be explained through some underlying data-generating mechanism that is incompletely observed and subject to selection effects. We additionally prove that EAMP chain graphs remain closed under marginalization, a valuable property ensuring that marginalized models maintain computational efficiency and simplicity.

text: This study introduces a novel approach for characterizing pattern sequences by establishing a ceiling value around which the aggregate measurement can fluctuate in sync with the outputs generated from individual patterns. Each time a node activates, it simultaneously acquires an activation/deactivation control signal. When a node becomes active, it transmits a control response calibrated to match the incoming signal's magnitude. A stronger or positive incoming signal permits the node to retain an activation control for the subsequent cycle. Conversely, a weaker or negative incoming signal allows it to retain a deactivation control for the subsequent cycle. When a node remains inactive, the feedback state remains unaffected and the node adopts the control signal from the most recent activation event of that same neuron. The ceiling value consequently designates the broadest or most comprehensive pattern collection, whereas the floor value corresponds to the genuine set of active patterns. When a pattern sequence recurs cyclically, the measurement oscillates between these two boundaries, facilitating improved identification and quantification across successive intervals. Experimental results demonstrate that altering the sequence arrangement yields distinct measurement collections that are similarly quantifiable.

text: During the 1870s, Boltzmann developed a logarithmic approach to quantifying how thermodynamic entropy relates to the microscopic state probabilities of a given system. Gibbs subsequently generalized his entropic framework across the full phase space of many-body systems, while von Neumann adapted it for quantum mechanical contexts. Shannon later employed this same formalism in information theory. For a discrete collection of $W$ microscopic states, the most basic version of this functional reads $S_{BG}= -k\sum_{i=1}^W p_i \ln p_i$ (where $k$ denotes a positive universal constant; {\it BG} refers to {\it Boltzmann-Gibbs}). From this expression emerges the framework of BG statistical mechanics. Although the BG approach has generated countless significant results and practical uses, its application to physical phenomena assumes that {\it ergodicity} holds true. Yet {\it how should we proceed when ergodicity and comparable basic assumptions break down?}—a situation that frequently occurs across numerous natural, engineered, and social systems exhibiting complexity. In 1988, researchers proposed generalizing BG statistical mechanics via a hierarchy of nonadditive entropy functions: $S_q=k\frac{1-\sum_{i=1}^W p_i^q}{q-1}$, which reduces to the traditional additive $S_{BG}$ entropy when $q \to 1$. The parameter $q$ should ideally be derived from underlying mechanical principles. Over the following thirty years, this concept developed extensively across the scientific world (consult Bibliography at \url{http://tsallis.cat.cbpf.br/biblio.htm}), yielding numerous theoretical predictions, experimental confirmations, and implementations spanning physics and beyond. As typically occurs with major {\it paradigm shifts}}, certain debate has understandably arisen within the field. This account surveys the current state of this framework, tracing its path from dynamical and thermodynamic underpinnings through to its most cutting-edge applications in physics.
label: ai

This algebraic framework contracts to the unique invariant point $q=1$, which aligns with the Boltzmann-Gibbs entropy formalism and classical statistical mechanics. When $q \ne 1$, an unbounded collection of $q$ values manifests, each potentially corresponding to distinct physical attributes of complex systems that admit description through $q$-statistics. The focus is on configurations comprising a small mass $m$ in rapid circular motion around a central body of mass $M$ at angular frequency $n_{\rm b}$, while that central body itself moves gradually in orbit around a far-away mass $M^{'}$ with much slower angular frequency $n_{\rm b}^{'}\ll n_{\rm b}$. Non-Keplerian modulations affecting both $m$ and $M$ are presumed to operate at frequencies significantly lower than either $n_{\rm b}$ or $n_{\rm b}^{'}$. Analytical formulas describing both Newtonian and post-Newtonian tidal perturbations to the orbit of $m$ are derived. The upcoming BepiColombo and JUICE space missions targeting Mercury and Ganymede respectively serve as observational test cases. The most pronounced signatures, ranging from roughly $0.1-0.5$ milliarcseconds annually (mas yr$^{-1}$), emerge for JUICE's Ganymede satellite probe. While refinements in space vehicle position tracking and orbit reconstruction could potentially achieve adequate precision, contamination from numerous additional orbital disturbances of both classical and relativistic nature would prove prohibitively large. An alternative approach using a purpose-built miniature celestial system mounted aboard an Earth-orbiting satellite is also examined. Post-Newtonian tidal precessions reaching $\approx 1-10^2$ mas yr$^{-1}$ appear feasible, yet the substantially stronger Newtonian tidal component would introduce considerable systematic error because present measurements of Earth's mass and gravitational constant carry roughly percent-level uncertainties.

A fundamental component of constraint programming frameworks involves constraint propagation algorithms. This work presents a unified and broadly applicable methodology for systematically describing various constraint propagation techniques. Our approach unfolds across two distinct phases. Initially, we formulate a general-purpose iterative procedure applicable to partial orders, establishing its validity within an abstract context. Subsequently, we specialize this procedure by introducing concrete partial orders and specific operations, thereby deriving concrete constraint propagation algorithms.

By leveraging the principles of commutativity and semi-commutativity, we demonstrate that the algorithms {\tt AC-3}, {\tt PC-2}, {\tt DAC}, and {\tt DPC}—which are designed to attain (directional) arc and (directional) path consistency—represent different instantiations of one overarching generic procedure. This work describes a simulated experimental environment that incorporates five distinct architectures for governing animats: a rule-driven approach, an action-oriented architecture, a representation-based framework, a connectionist model, and a Braitenberg-style design. By conducting a series of comparative experiments, I demonstrate that no single architecture emerges as universally superior; rather, each excels within particular domains and specific situations.

The selected architectures, despite their relative simplicity, embody divergent methodologies for investigating cognitive phenomena. Drawing on experimental findings as an empirical tool for philosophical inquiry, I argue that no single methodology for examining cognition can claim universal superiority, given that each carries both strengths and limitations—each illuminates distinct facets of cognition from particular vantage points. This observation bears relevance to ongoing scholarly discussions regarding which methodologies represent the "correct" approach to cognition: while all methodologies possess merit, none achieves complete adequacy on its own. I offer observations regarding how cognition might be conceptualized when drawing insights from the full range of investigative methods, and I present a basic typology distinguishing various forms of cognitive activity.
label: ai

text: This work tackles the challenge of categorizing instances where feature characteristics depend on contextual factors, particularly when the evaluation dataset contains contexts absent from the training data. The paper first establishes a formal framework for the issue, followed by the presentation of broad methodologies aimed at boosting classifier effectiveness in such scenarios. These methodologies are subsequently evaluated across three distinct application areas. The initial application involves identifying faults in gas turbine engines, where the objective is to pinpoint a defective engine under one set of conditions—for example, warm temperatures—despite the malfunction being previously observed only under different conditions, such as cold temperatures. The second application pertains to automated speech processing, where speaker identity constitutes the contextual dimension. Here, the challenge involves identifying spoken utterances from speakers not included in the original training data. The third application concerns healthcare prediction, specifically forecasting patient survival outcomes in hepatitis cases, with patient age serving as the contextual variable. Across all three applications, accounting for context leads to markedly superior classification outcomes.

text: We propose an approach for categorizing new evidence within the Dempster-Shafer framework. Our technique builds upon earlier research involving the grouping of ambiguous evidence and its transformation into more specific forms. Rather than performing complete re-clustering whenever new evidence arrives, our approach compares incoming data against representative cluster prototypes for rapid assignment. This strategy offers computational efficiency, requiring O(M * N) operations per new evidence item, where M represents the upper bound on subset count and N denotes the number of selected prototypes per subset. Notably, this complexity remains constant regardless of how much evidence has previously been collected. The values of M and N are ordinarily determined in advance and tailored to the particular problem domain.

text: This work examines a Dempster-Shafer theoretical challenge wherein 2**n - 1 items of evidence are partitioned into n groups through a neural network architecture. The partitioning process involves reducing a metaconflict objective function. An earlier approach utilizing iterative optimization techniques was previously introduced. Nevertheless, handling large-scale instances requires algorithms with reduced computational overhead. The neural architecture proved to be substantially more efficient than iterative optimization when addressing larger-scale cases. Although the neural architecture exhibited faster metaconflict escalation relative to iterative optimization for intermediate-sized instances, the ratio of metaconflict to both cluster count and evidence quantity remained acceptable. Across ten independent trials, the neural architecture succeeded in identifying global optima for problem instances up to six clusters.
label: ai

text: This work builds upon prior research in Dempster-Shafer theory ["Fast Dempster-Shafer Clustering Using a Neural Network Structure," in Proc. Seventh Int. Conf. An earlier approach employed iterative optimization techniques; although the neural approach demonstrated substantially faster computational speeds than its iterative counterpart, it produced inferior clustering results on average. A metaconflict objective was minimized to accomplish this task. Here, we propose an approach that determines both cluster assignments and the optimal number of clusters concurrently as the neural mechanism iterates. Neuron output values are interpreted as measures indicating the degree to which individual pieces of evidence correspond to particular clusters. Using these values, we construct a probabilistic model over possible cluster quantities. Throughout successive iterations, this probabilistic framework progressively crystallizes into a definitive cluster count. This progressively refined determination is reintroduced to the neural structure at every iteration step, thereby shaping the subsequent clustering outcomes.

text: This paper introduces a technique for tracking at the report level that employs Dempster-Shafer clustering with Potts spin neural networks. The approach operates by progressively merging groups of newly received reports into pre-existing tracks, with each track maintaining its own associated cluster. New reports are assigned to clusters, and these clusters undergo continuous reorganization of their historical reports to optimize both internal cohesion and alignment with their respective tracks. As time advances, earlier reports gradually transition from the cluster into the established track, occurring at the same pace that fresh reports enter the cluster. This method of integrating reports into current tracks enables simultaneous consideration of both the established track history and anticipated track trajectories, as reflected by more recent reports held within the cluster. The outcome is superior report-to-track matching quality. In practical terms, this strategy surpasses the computational demands of clustering all obtainable reports while simultaneously delivering improved report-to-track associations relative to conventional sequential matching techniques.
label: ai

text: This work investigates predictive modeling through monotone Kolmogorov complexity, specifically via Km=-log m, which implements universal deterministic MDL in a single-part framework. The quantity m bears striking similarity to Solomonoff's measure M, which demonstrates strong predictive performance across both stochastic and deterministic settings, with effectiveness gauged by posterior convergence and loss metrics. However, despite this resemblance to M, evaluating m's predictive accuracy proves challenging due to limited understanding of how closely their posterior distributions align—a crucial factor for making predictions. Our analysis reveals that when environments are deterministic and computable, m exhibits convergence in both its "posterior" and associated losses, though we can only establish fast convergence along observed sequences; behavior outside these sequences remains ambiguous. When considering stochastic environments, neither the posterior distributions nor the losses generally achieve convergence.

text:
This paper proposes that "information compression via concurrent alignment, merging and retrieval" (ICMAUS) offers a unified approach for expressing linguistic syntax in an uncomplicated way, while enabling clear and straightforward implementations of natural language comprehension and generation.

The ICMAUS methodology is realized through a computational system called SP61. The article outlines the structure and functioning of this system and demonstrates its practical application through a basic illustration of how it processes natural language.

Despite what appears to be a seeming contradiction in the notion of 'expansion through compression', the ICMAUS approach, without requiring any adjustments, can generate sentences by decoding a compacted representation of that sentence. Output examples from SP61 serve as evidence of this capability.

The paper further includes four additional illustrations spanning different linguistic phenomena: analysis of a French utterance and three cases involving English modal auxiliaries. These demonstrations illustrate how both the ICMAUS methodology and the SP61 implementation elegantly handle grammar's 'situation-dependent' characteristics in a straightforward and economical fashion.

text: Managing urban traffic represents a challenging endeavor requiring coordination across numerous stakeholders. Conventional strategies seek to fine-tune traffic signals based on anticipated traffic volume and layout patterns. Rather than viewing traffic management as an optimization challenge, it should be understood as an adaptation challenge. By operating under elementary principles and without requiring inter-signal communication, traffic lights achieve autonomous coordination capable of responding flexibly to shifting traffic circumstances, thereby decreasing congestion duration, minimizing idle vehicles, and boosting travel velocity.

text: In semantic networks, edges connecting any two vertices carry specific meaning. However, identifying which vertices hold the greatest "importance" proves challenging, since different relationship types may be weighted differently depending on subjective judgment. Consequently, scholarship on semantic network metrics has predominantly emphasized context-dependent approaches (meaning rankings specified by users). Additionally, numerous existing semantic network metrics assess semantic relationships (that is, sequences of directed connections linking pairs of vertices) rather than the vertices themselves. This work introduces a technique for computing semantically significant metrics founded on primary eigenvectors—such as eigenvector centrality and PageRank—within semantic networks by employing an adapted form of the random walker approach derived from Markov chain theory. Within this framework, random walkers are limited by grammatical constraints, with the grammar functioning as a structure defined by the user that establishes what the ultimate vertex ranking signifies. The concepts explored here are situated within the context of the Resource Description Framework (RDF), which forms part of the broader Semantic Web initiative.

text:
Recent years have witnessed the deployment of multiple local search methods to tackle machine scheduling challenges characterized by competing objectives. The primary goal of these approaches centers on discovering the complete collection of Pareto-efficient solutions. A critical factor in achieving success with such problems involves the careful selection of an appropriate neighbourhood structure. However, the relationship between fitness landscape characteristics and problem-solving effectiveness remains inadequately understood.

This study examines neighbourhood operators employed in multi-objective flow shop scheduling. Twelve distinct criterion combinations were tested experimentally. To ensure reliable findings, the researchers selected small-scale problem instances with known optimal solutions. Results from statistical analysis indicate that individual neighbourhood operators cannot uniformly locate every Pareto-efficient solution. Nonetheless, when the algorithm incorporated a stochastic variable neighbourhood search strategy, performance improved markedly through this hybrid approach.

text: This work presents a local search metaheuristic designed for tackling multi-objective optimization challenges. The methodology leverages two fundamental concepts within heuristic search: exploration of diverse neighborhood structures for intensive searching, and exploration beyond current regions through random perturbations and repeated refinement cycles. text:
This paper introduces a computational framework designed to address multi-objective production scheduling challenges (MOOPPS). Through a collection of embedded metaheuristic techniques, it becomes feasible to identify Pareto-efficient solutions or reasonable approximations thereof. Selection of an optimal solution from among the discovered efficient alternatives is facilitated by an integrated component utilizing the aspiration interactive method (AIM). Through an iterative process in which the decision maker specifies successive aspiration targets, the framework converges toward a single preferred solution.

text: A covariance graph, also referred to as a bi-directed graph, represents a probability distribution $p$ through an undirected graph $G$ where any two nodes are connected precisely when their associated random variables exhibit marginal dependence within $p$. We establish that this graphical method possesses both soundness and completeness under appropriate conditions. We contend that our requirements are sufficiently general for practical purposes. Notably, this applies to all standard Gaussian distributions, which indeed satisfy these conditions.

text: Detecting intrusions within wireless ad hoc networks presents significant difficulties stemming from several inherent characteristics: these networks continuously shift their structural configurations, possess no centralized locations for traffic monitoring, employ vulnerable infrastructure-based protocols, and depend on unreliable wireless transmissions. The security landscape of such networks is complicated by their open-access nature, frequent topology modifications, dependence on collaborative mechanisms, absence of centralized observation capabilities, and poorly defined security boundaries. This paper introduces a collaborative, decentralized intrusion detection framework utilizing node clustering to mitigate network security weaknesses and enable reliable attack identification. The system operates through a flexible hierarchical model where nodes collect intrusion information and progressively consolidate, compress, and examines this data as it propagates upward toward cluster heads. When needed, cluster heads from neighboring clusters establish connections to perform joint intrusion detection efforts. For transmitting intrusion-related communications, the architecture leverages mobile agents because of their capability for efficient lightweight processing and their appropriateness for collaborative intrusion detection tasks. Performance evaluations demonstrate that the proposed framework operates with both high effectiveness and operational efficiency.

text: Disjunctive hybrid probability logic programs (DHPP) represent a form of declarative programming that has demonstrated utility for handling diverse probability reasoning problems. A significant limitation, however, is the absence of probability aggregates—such as expected values—within the DHPP framework, which restricts the ability to express numerous compelling applications in an elegant and straightforward manner. This work addresses this gap by generalizing DHPP to incorporate unrestricted probability aggregates. text: A recent technique called Compressed Counting (CC) [22] enables practitioners to compute the ath frequency moments across streaming data for cases where 0 < a <= 2. Since Shannon entropy can be derived from particular transformations of ath frequency moments as a approaches 1, CC offers a pathway for entropy calculation. In networking contexts, tracking Shannon entropy serves as a valuable mechanism for identifying anomalies, including threats like DDoS attacks, particularly within expansive network infrastructures. This work introduces an enhanced version of CC that delivers notable performance gains, with the most dramatic improvements occurring as a approaches 1 from below. To illustrate, at a = 0.99, the proposed method achieves approximately a 100-fold reduction in variance compared to the original approach. By substantially lowering computational demands, this enhancement would expand CC's applicability in practical settings where Shannon entropy estimation is required. Additionally, when a = 0.5, this improved algorithm achieves statistical optimality.

text: The author introduced Neutrosophic Over-/Under-/Off-Set and -Logic in 1995, with initial publication occurring in 2007. These concepts represent extensions of the foundational neutrosophic set framework, which can be classified into three categories: Neutrosophic Overset {occurring when at least one neutrosophic component exceeds 1}, Neutrosophic Underset {occurring when at least one neutrosophic component falls below 0}, and Neutrosophic Offset {occurring when neutrosophic components deviate from the [0, 1] range, with some surpassing 1 and others dropping below 0}. Unlike conventional fuzzy set/logic, intuitionistic fuzzy set/logic, or standard/approximate probability—which restrict all values within [0, 1]—this advancement acknowledges that actual applications frequently demand neutrosophic components outside conventional bounds. Real-world scenarios provide compelling justification; an employee engaged in overtime work warrants a membership grade exceeding 1, whereas an individual whose contributions prove detrimental rather than valuable to an organization rightfully receives a membership below 0. Consequently, the framework of Neutrosophic Logic/Measure/Probability/Statistics and related constructs underwent parallel expansions, yielding Neutrosophic Over-/Under-/Off-Logic, -Measure, -Probability, -Statistics and analogous systems [Smarandache, 2007].

text: While Proof Theory has witnessed notable progress, the idea of what constitutes a proof verifier has remarkably received scant examination. Specifically, scholars in mathematics could benefit from investigating various categories of proof verifiers—including humans, algorithms, oracles, collective entities, and artificial superintelligences—as formal mathematical constructs. Such an inquiry might illuminate their fundamental characteristics, capabilities and constraints (especially concerning human mathematicians), their minimal and maximal computational requirements, and complications arising from self-reference and self-validation mechanisms. We present an elementary taxonomy of verifier types alongside basic treatment of both established and unresolved issues within this significant field. Our primary offering consists of formally characterizing the phenomenon of unverifiability, positioning this work as a foundational reference across verification domains spanning theorem proving, software verification, and artificial intelligence systems.

text: According to Gray's 2015 work, the Fermi paradox (FP) should not be considered a genuine paradox and represents a misleading designation. Gray further contended that Fermi himself did not originate this concept—his casual luncheon conversation actually focused on the practical challenges of space travel rather than the question of alien life. Gray credits Hart and Tipler as the true architects of the paradox, while simultaneously proposing that it lacks scientific legitimacy and therefore has no place in SETI-related discussions. However, these claims suffer from weak reasoning, lack historical rigor, and in certain cases depend on overly rigid and ungenerous interpretations of available data. I propose three counterarguments instead: (i) Regardless of Gray's claims, the historical attribution of concepts bears no connection to their intellectual validity. (ii) The Great Silence paradox represents a natural extension of FP, making any attempt to treat them as distinct concepts neither theoretically sound nor empirically justified. (iii) Contrary to Gray's central thesis, FP has actually intensified in recent times as astrobiology has advanced and expanded our understanding.

text: The capacity of hypergraphs to support belief updating through the Shenoy/Shafer framework via localized computations forms the central focus of this investigation. text:
This paper examines multiple methods from existing literature for organizing and breaking down Dempster-Shafer collective belief functions, with particular focus on their ability to represent independence relationships—given the perspective that belief functions extend the Bayesian probability framework.

The analysis shows that logical networks developed by Zhu and Lee and the directed acyclic graphs proposed by Smets both fail to adequately model the statistical dependencies and independencies found in Bayesian networks as described by Pearl. In contrast, the hypergraph model introduced by Shenoy and Shafer successfully portrays how Bayesian belief functions factor according to Bayesian network structure, yet the authors maintain that representing variable independence in belief function contexts is unnecessary.

Cano and colleagues challenge the Shenoy-Shafer hypergraph approach specifically because it omits variable independence representation. However, their proposed alternative framework cannot decompose all belief functions that are decomposable within the Shenoy-Shafer system.

A competing strategy presented in the Klopotek framework achieves a synthesis of these competing perspectives. It maintains the simplest possible factorization for belief functions under the Shenoy-Shafer scheme, while simultaneously capturing all variable independencies recognized by the Cano approach—plus additional ones beyond their scope.
label: ai

text:
The Dempster-Shafer Theory, alternatively referred to as the Mathematical Theory of Evidence (DST), serves as a fundamental framework for conducting inference when information is characterized by differing degrees of specificity. Despite considerable scholarly attention devoted to developing this theory since its inception, numerous unresolved issues persist. Among the most significant of these unresolved matters is how frequencies relate to the Mathematical Theory of Evidence. A persistent criticism holds that the theory systematically excludes frequencies from its conceptual scope. This critique carries substantial weight for two principal reasons: (1) empirical studies cannot be conducted to validate the effectiveness of DST-based representations of phenomena in the actual world relative to observed evidence, and (2) empirical observations cannot form the basis for designing appropriate models of belief.

  This work presents a frequency-based framework for understanding DST that effectively rebuts the preceding objection. A key implication of this approach is the feasibility of creating computational procedures that automatically construct DST belief models on the basis of empirical evidence. We introduce three distinct procedures suited to different types of belief model configurations: those organized as tree structures, those arranged as poly-tree structures, and those comprising unrestricted network topologies.
label: ai

text:
The graphoidal nature of Shenoy's independence concept within Dempster-Shafer belief function theory is confirmed in this work, building on findings from {Shenoy:94}. Shenoy had established that his independence formulation exhibits graphoidal characteristics when applied to positive normal valuations.

However, the constraint that valuations must be strictly positive and normal creates significant limitations, filtering out numerous DS belief functions from consideration. This restriction is particularly problematic for probabilistic belief functions. We show that the positivity constraint can be relaxed by instead requiring that the commonality function takes non-zero values on singleton sets, while still maintaining the graphoidal properties of independence among belief function variables. Consequently, probabilistic belief functions that have singleton sets as their sole focal elements satisfy the graphoidal properties required for independence.
label: ai

text: We establish in this work that certain Gaussian structural equation models featuring correlated errors with uniform variance are identifiable on the basis of their corresponding Gaussian distributions. We specifically demonstrate identifiability for those Gaussian structural equation models expressible as Andersson-Madigan-Perlman chain graphs (Andersson et al., 2001). Originally conceived to encode independence structures, these chain graphs prove equally useful for encoding causal models with additive noise (Peña, 2016). A key implication of our findings is that such causal models may be determined using only observational data. Our contribution extends prior work by Peters and Bühlmann (2014), who restricted their analysis to uncorrelated errors with uniform variance. The appropriateness of assuming equal error variances warrants evaluation within specific application contexts.

text: We investigate connections between the zeta distribution and algorithmic information theory through a novel framework for studying transfer learning. The distribution of programs is well-approximated by a zeta distribution with a parameter close to $1$. Training data is treated as a stochastic process in our framework. We derive upper bounds on learning time for a training sequence and examine its entropy rates, relying on an oracle capable of solving the transfer learning problem. Empirical observations support the use of power-law distributions to characterize natural phenomena. Four distinct sequence models are introduced. The random typing model mirrors the no-free lunch theorem, where transfer learning provides no advantage. The zeta process independently draws programs according to the zeta distribution. A genetically-inspired model leverages a repository of sub-program components. An evolutionary zeta process generates mutations drawn from the zeta distribution. Study of these evolution-based stochastic processes reveals that artificial intelligence may be achievable in biological systems, providing a counterargument to no-free lunch type limitations.

text: Though Wille introduced formal contexts and concept lattices merely a decade ago, these frameworks have already demonstrated considerable value across numerous practical domains, particularly in knowledge representation and data analysis. text: The present section explores how institutions offer a structured methodology for organizing and preserving ontologies. Joseph Goguen and Rod Burstall pioneered and formalized the institutional theory framework. Grounded in category-theoretic principles, this theoretical framework treats ontologies as formal logical systems or particular logic variants. By leveraging category-theoretic concepts such as fibrations and indexed categories, the institutional theory enables the construction of logical frameworks. Institutional theory synthesizes the lattice-based methodology from Ganter and Wille's Formal Concept Analysis with Barwise and Seligman's approach to distributed logical systems known as Information Flow. Within its local contexts, the institutional paradigm integrates Sowa's conception of a theory lattice derived from knowledge representation studies. Applied through the Information Flow Framework—developed as part of the IEEE Standard Upper Ontology initiative—the institutional methodology serves practical purposes in evaluating, merging semantically, and administering ontologies. This section elaborates on the fundamental concepts underlying the institutional perspective on ontologies with meticulous thoroughness and precision.

text: When multiple communities engaged in different forms of discourse exchange ontologies, they gain the ability to evaluate how their respective information frameworks compare with those of other groups employing compatible terminology and conceptual foundations. Such exchanges promote interoperability across digital repositories of knowledge. This work illustrates how ontology exchange can be characterized mathematically using the Information Flow conceptual framework. Rather than explicitly depicting exchange, Information Flow does so implicitly by means of a hierarchical structure of ontology extensions paired with equivalent types that function as synonyms—distinct ontologies attain mutual comprehension of terms and concepts by each building upon a shared, foundational ontology. Within a framework where individual communities maintain their own ontologies expressed in Information Flow logic, a universally accessible ontology represented as an Information Flow theory, and connections from this universal ontology to each community's specific ontology represented as Information Flow theory mappings, the paper contends that ontological exchange concentrates itself in a meta-ontology reflecting the web of community interactions. The paper further demonstrates that such a meta-ontology can be mechanically derived as the combination of all community ontologies—specifically, the combined total of these ontologies reduced by their mutual ontological elements.

text: This paper addresses temporal equilibrium in parallel-processor scheduling for composite modular operations, a problem that shares characteristics with just-in-time manufacturing frameworks. The discussion includes a practical case study involving modular construction, where the primary goal centers on synchronizing the output of standardized building components with the subsequent assembly activities performed by multiple work crews. This approach employs a metaheuristic technique grounded in iterative local improvements. text: This investigation examines the possibility of quantifying rational behavior. Rational behavior encompasses the application of exhaustive data, processed through an idealized neural or mechanical substrate, toward maximizing outcomes. Computing rational behavior requires establishing metrics for three distinct dimensions: the comprehensiveness of available information, the fidelity of the neural or mechanical processing system, and the degree to which the decision process achieves optimization. The model's rationality—referring to either the biological or mechanical system—is assessed through the anticipated correctness of its output. The optimization process's rationality is gauged by comparing the obtained result to the theoretical maximum. A decision's total rationality derives from multiplying the model's rationality by the optimization process's rationality. The research concludes that rational behavior can be quantified specifically within the domain of convex optimization challenges.

text: Finding effective linear predictors using minimal variables—that is, reducing the number of features in their support—is a central goal of sparse supervised learning techniques. Rather than directly tackling this combinatorial variable selection challenge, researchers typically reformulate it as a convex problem by substituting the cardinality constraint with its convex hull (the least restrictive convex upper bound), typically the L1-norm. This work extends beyond simple cardinality by examining broader classes of set-functions that can encode domain expertise and structural regularities prevalent across practical problems. Specifically, we demonstrate that when set-functions are nondecreasing and submodular, their convex hull emerges naturally from the Lovász extension, a standard technique in submodular optimization theory. This construction yields a collection of polyhedral norms, which we furnish with general-purpose computational methods (including subdifferentials and proximity operators) alongside formal guarantees (such as requirements for exact variable identification or asymptotic behavior in high dimensions). Through judicious choices of submodular functions, we can reinterpret classical norms derived from rank-based statistics or group penalties with overlapping group structures; additionally, we introduce novel norms applicable as structured priors in machine learning contexts.

text: Positive definite kernels enable both supervised and unsupervised learning approaches to leverage expansive and potentially unbounded feature spaces while maintaining computational efficiency proportional to sample size. Typically, this is achieved by constraining prediction models using Euclidean or Hilbertian norm penalties. This work investigates an alternative approach: regularizing prediction functions through sparsity-promoting norms, including the l1-norm and block l1-norm variants. We propose that when a kernel can be expressed as an extensive combination of elementary basis kernels arranged within a directed acyclic graph structure, kernel selection becomes feasible via hierarchical multiple kernel learning in time polynomial relative to the number of selected kernels. This methodology naturally extends to nonlinear feature selection; our comprehensive empirical evaluation using synthetic data and real-world benchmarks from the UCI repository demonstrates that effectively navigating high-dimensional feature spaces using sparsity-promoting norms achieves competitive predictive results.

text: I examine self-directed or self-taught neural networks competing in minimax-style game-theoretic scenarios. (i) Adversarial Curiosity (AC, 1990) employs two competing networks. A first network acquires the capability to stochastically produce outputs, while a second network develops the ability to anticipate consequences of those outputs. Each network seeks to minimize what the other seeks to maximize. (ii) Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs, 2010-2014) represent a specific instantiation of AC wherein an output's consequence equals 1 when that output belongs to a specified set, and 0 otherwise. (iii) Predictability Minimization (PM, 1990s) characterizes data by means of a neural encoder functioning against a neural predictor that works on encoded components—the encoder maximizes what the predictor minimizes. We address and rectify a prior assertion claiming that PM operates independently from minimax competition.

text: This work introduces a comprehensive framework for understanding prediction errors that arise when algorithms for supervised learning are evaluated through cross-validation on sample data. The examples themselves are represented using attribute-value notation with non-numeric attribute values. The cross-validation methodology involves partitioning data into a training subset, where the target attribute values are accessible to the algorithm, and a testing subset, where these values remain hidden from the learner. The framework reveals that cross-validation error consists of two distinct elements: performance gaps on the training data (lack of accuracy) and vulnerability to perturbations in the data (lack of robustness). This framework is subsequently extended to examine the role of voting mechanisms in proximity-based learning systems. When making predictions for a test instance, these algorithms typically determine the target attribute by aggregating votes from the k most proximate training instances. Although voting mechanisms are designed to enhance robustness (reducing susceptibility to noise), theoretical investigation uncovers situations where voting might actually undermine robustness. The framework provides strategies for reducing overall cross-validation error through careful management of voting procedures, ensuring they maintain robustness while preserving or improving prediction accuracy.

text: Understanding text meaning through semantic composition involves combining the meanings of constituent words. Conversely, semantic decomposition entails grasping a single word's meaning by breaking it down into its underlying aspects (dimensions, elements, features). We employ a distributional semantic framework wherein words are encoded as context vectors. Recent research has addressed the identification of compositional and decomposional patterns, yet our work pursues the more challenging generative task. To streamline our approach, we concentrate on two-word noun phrases and single nouns. One test of semantic composition requires producing a noun unigram that matches the meaning of a given noun-modifier pair (for instance, generating "sockeye" from "red salmon"). A corresponding test of semantic decomposition requires generating a noun-modifier pair equivalent in meaning to a given unigram (producing "brandy glass" from "snifter"). Given a vocabulary spanning roughly 73,000 unigrams sourced from WordNet, the challenge involves selecting from 73,000 possible unigram replacements for a bigram, as well as 5,300,000,000 (73,000 squared) feasible bigram alternatives for a unigram. Our approach produces ordered candidate lists via a two-stage procedure. An efficient unsupervised method first produces preliminary candidates, which a more computationally intensive supervised approach subsequently improves. text: Joint clustering across several tasks can be enhanced by considering how these tasks relate to one another. Previous approaches to multitask clustering have predominantly employed generative methods and have not been structured as convex problems. This work introduces two convex formulations for Discriminative Multitask Clustering (DMTC) to overcome these limitations. We begin by establishing a Bayesian framework for DMTC, followed by the development of two convex optimization objectives grounded in this framework. The first objective merges convex multitask feature learning with convex Multiclass Maximum Margin Clustering (M3C) to discover shared feature representations across tasks. The second objective integrates convex multitask relationship learning with M3C to uncover underlying task relationships. Both optimization problems are addressed using a unified approach based on the efficient cutting-plane technique. Validation through experiments on synthetic data and real-world datasets shows that these proposed methods perform effectively.

text: We present a novel approach to kernel learning grounded in nonparametric density estimation. Our methodology employs multiple k-centroids clustering operations, where each operation independently picks data samples and feature subsets to serve as centroids, then derives a one-hot encoding scheme via nearest-neighbor-based optimization. This produces a compressed feature representation for every input sample. We subsequently form a nonlinear kernel matrix based on these compressed representations. The proposed technique offers two key benefits: first, it demonstrates robustness across parameter choices, enabling effective performance without extensive hyperparameter optimization; second, it maintains computational simplicity. We believe this approach could prove valuable across numerous machine learning contexts involving sparse coding or kernel matrices. text: Conventional approaches to quantum state tomography demand measurement counts that scale exponentially as the qubit count n increases. text:
A fundamental challenge in machine learning involves the tradeoff between bias and variance: making a model more sophisticated typically enhances its ability to capture training patterns, yet this often degrades its capacity to generalize to new instances. Paradoxically, biological brains demonstrate remarkable capability in solving intricate pattern-matching problems across diverse domains. Given these seemingly contradictory facts, researchers including Geman et al have proposed that cortical tissue must contain innate "generic mechanisms for representation"—useful biases encoded directly into neural architecture.

This paper introduces a bias supporting collaborative learning that is simultaneously grounded in biological principles and supported by rigorous theoretical foundations.

text:
This work focuses on chain graphs according to the Andersson-Madigan-Perlman (AMP) framework. We propose a constraint-based method for identifying an AMP chain graph to which a specified probability distribution remains faithful. Additionally, we demonstrate that Meek's conjecture cannot be generalized to AMP chain graphs, which limits the creation of computationally tractable and valid score+search learning methods when weaker conditions than faithfulness are assumed.

We further introduce a novel class of graphical models incorporating undirected and bidirected connections. We refer to this class as maximal covariance-concentration graphs (MCCGs) since it subsumes both covariance and concentration graphs. Notably, any MCCG can be understood as what emerges from eliminating particular nodes in an AMP chain graph. We formulate global, local, and pairwise Markov properties for MCCGs and establish that these properties are equivalent. We determine the conditions under which two MCCGs possess the same Markov equivalence and demonstrate that each Markov equivalence class of MCCGs contains a unique representative. We put forth a constraint-based procedure for learning an MCCG that a given probability distribution proves faithful to.

Finally, we provide a graphical approach for extracting independencies from an MCCG of a probability distribution exhibiting the graphoid characteristics, weak transitivity, and composition. We establish that this approach holds both soundness and completeness in a defined manner.

text: Under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) framework, the model yielding the most concise encoding of both data and model parameters is preferred. We demonstrate that when dealing with a countable set of candidate models, MDL-based forecasts converge toward the genuine distribution with considerable strength. Our findings apply universally across contexts. The analysis requires no assumptions regarding independence, ergodicity, stationarity, identifiability, or any other properties of the model collection. More precisely, we establish that given any countable collection of models, the probability distributions identified through MDL (or maximum a posteriori) estimation progressively align with the true underlying measure belonging to that collection, as measured by total variation distance. We address how these theoretical insights extend to dependent data scenarios such as sequential prediction tasks, supervised feature-based learning, and decision-making under uncertainty.

text: Collections of data points existing in two- or three-dimensional space constitute point clouds. To date, most kernel-based approaches to learning from point collections have overlooked the particular geometric symmetries and real-world requirements inherent in point cloud applications within visual computing and rendering. This work introduces generalizations of graph kernels tailored for point clouds, enabling the application of kernel techniques to diverse objects including geometric shapes, sketch-like line patterns, and arbitrary three-dimensional point collections. To construct computationally tractable and expressively powerful kernels with minimal tunable hyperparameters, we leverage kernels operating on covariance matrix pairs and their decompositions via probabilistic graphical models. We establish polynomial-time algorithms based on dynamic programming and demonstrate their utility through tasks involving classification of handwritten numerals and recognition of Chinese script characters using limited labeled data.

text: Walley's Imprecise Dirichlet Model (IDM) extends traditional Dirichlet approaches to categorical independent and identically distributed observations by incorporating a collection of prior distributions. This framework successfully addresses key limitations that plague alternative uncertainty quantification methods. However, practical implementation requires computationally efficient algorithms for determining the imprecise-robust sets and associated intervals. The primary aim of this research is to establish precise, conservative, and approximate robust and credible interval estimators within the IDM framework, applicable across a broad spectrum of statistical estimands spanning entropy and mutual information measures.

text: A correlation-resemblance kernel, abbreviated as CoRE kernel, represents a particular kernel formulation. In numerous practical domains such as computer vision, datasets typically exhibit characteristics of high dimensionality, sparsity, and non-binary structure. We introduce a pair of nonlinear CoRE kernel variants tailored for sparse non-binary data and validate their utility through classification benchmarks. The appeal of CoRE kernels lies in their straightforward design and absence of hyperparameters requiring calibration. Nevertheless, the computational overhead of training nonlinear kernel support vector machines can be prohibitively expensive regarding both runtime and memory consumption, rendering them impractical for large-scale deployments in real-world industrial contexts such as information retrieval systems. To enhance the applicability of our proposed CoRE kernels, we devise elementary stochastic hashing techniques capable of converting nonlinear kernel formulations into their linear equivalents.
